input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is an **industrial robot system** (specifically referenced as part of the MABI MAX 100 platform) integrated with a **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller**, designed for high-precision motion control in manufacturing environments. Key mechanical components include **motors, gearboxes, and encoders**, which enable multi-physics-aware monitoring of joint friction, critical for improving motion accuracy and predictive maintenance. The operational domain focuses on real-time data aggregation, friction analysis, and fault detection in automated assembly or manufacturing processes, leveraging contextualized sensor data traces for operational insights.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides the following specialized services:
  - **Friction Identification**: Continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction in real-time, leveraging aggregated contextual data traces from the physical twin.
  - **Friction Loss Detection**: Understanding and detecting anomalies in friction-related parameters (e.g., torque, velocity deviations) to ensure motion accuracy.
  - **Visualization Dashboard**: A user interface for monitoring friction-related metrics, enabling operators to visualize system health and performance trends.
  The target users include **maintenance technicians** and **production supervisors**, who rely on these services to optimize system performance and prevent faults.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on:
  - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)**: A standardized framework for integrating robot components (motors, encoders, controllers) into a unified digital representation, enabling interoperability with Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl.
  - **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl Controller**: Core hardware/software stack for real-time control, interfacing with the Digital Twin via MQTT-based communication for data exchange.
  - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) Tools**: SysML for architecture description (component/connector modeling, state machines) and MathWorks Simulink for behavior simulation and validation.
  - **MQTT Protocol**: Lightweight messaging protocol for bidirectional data flow between the physical robot and the Digital Twin, ensuring low-latency contextual data aggregation.
  - **Contextual Data Aggregation**: Leverages digital shadow principles (one-way data flow from physical sensors to digital traces) to preprocess and abstract sensor data for DT analysis.
  Additional enablers include **real-time simulation engines** (e.g., embedded in SINUMERIK 840D sl) for closed-loop validation of DT predictions.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided documents focus on a **single Digital Shadow instance** for a specific physical object (e.g., a robot axis), where the **hierarchical structure** is derived from Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **SysML-based architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **AADL or MontiArc**. The system does not explicitly describe **multiple Digital Twin instances** or decentralized architectures. Coordination mechanisms are implicitly handled through **component & connector perspectives** in ADLs, but no explicit mention of **multi-instance coordination** or **scope of responsibilities** is available. Thus, this characteristic is **Not in Document** for multiplicities beyond the single-system context.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin (DT) system** is conceptualized as an extension of the **Digital Shadow**, which operates as a **one-way data flow** from the physical system to a digital representation. The **orchestration** of the DT system is grounded in **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, leveraging **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** to model behavior. The **system-wide coordination** is implicitly supported by **active software components** that process data from digital shadows, enabling autonomous operations and real-time synchronization. The **resource management** is implied through the integration of **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) and **general-purpose languages** (e.g., **C++/Java**) to refine and validate DT models. The absence of explicit orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) in the documents suggests a **hybrid centralized-decentralized approach**, where core coordination is handled by MBSE frameworks and ADLs, supplemented by tool-specific workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
A Digital Twin (DT) system in this context leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks to structure computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key model types include:
- **Geometric models**: Represented through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **AADL** or **SysML Interaction Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define system components, their interfaces, and interconnections (e.g., component & connector perspectives).
- **Behavioral models**: Enabled by **MontiArc Automata** or **SysML state machines** (v1.7) for detailed system behavior, often integrated with simulation tools such as **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation methodologies (e.g., [29], [30]). These models are further refined using code in general-purpose languages like **C++** or **Java**.

Data components are managed through **heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives**, requiring integration across disparate modeling notations and tools. The Digital Twin constellation aggregates **contextual data traces** (as per the **Digital Shadow reference model**, [32]), which are abstracted and processed via active components (e.g., [35]–[37]) to enable bidirectional communication. The **Digital Shadow** itself is defined as a **one-way automated data flow** from physical objects to digital representations, emphasizing passive aggregation and abstraction for specific purposes (e.g., manufacturing).

Model relationships are implicitly governed by **methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**, which systematically organize models into hierarchical layers. Data management involves **interoperability challenges** due to tool heterogeneity, necessitating standardized interfaces (e.g., ADLs) and collaborative workflows among stakeholders.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical actuation system in this Digital Twin constellation is driven by a **permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM)**, integrated with a **gear transmission mechanism** (specifically a **cycloidal gearbox**). The motor’s permanent magnet design ensures high efficiency and torque density, while the cycloidal gearbox provides precise positional control and torque amplification.

The actuation system’s control capabilities are enabled through **closed-loop feedback mechanisms**, where two **position encoders** (one mounted directly on the motor and another behind the gearbox) monitor displacement to minimize backlash and positional uncertainty. This arrangement enhances accuracy and reduces errors in the physical actuation path. Safety constraints are implicitly managed through the sensor-embedded control loop, ensuring real-time validation of operational limits.

The addressing mechanism for actuation is not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the system’s modular design suggests a hierarchical control structure where the PMSM and gearbox are managed via a centralized control interface (likely embedded in the digital twin’s simulation or real-time control framework). Actuation ranges and limitations are inferred from the gearbox’s mechanical specifications (e.g., torque capacity, speed limits), though exact values are not provided in the context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The **sensing infrastructure** includes **two position encoders** distributed spatially—one associated with the **motor** and another with the **gearbox**. These sensors provide **spatial distribution** and **measurement capabilities** tied to physical positioning. The data collection mechanism is implied as **real-time feedback** for control loops, though exact sampling rates, transmission rates, or precision specifications are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: While the documents emphasize **model construction** via MBSE (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and **data aggregation** for digital shadows, **fidelity and validity considerations** are not explicitly addressed. The absence of validation/verification (V&V) protocols, uncertainty quantification methods, or error correction mechanisms implies these are **implicitly handled** within broader MBSE frameworks (e.g., [18–22]) but are not specified in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** for this Digital Shadow involves a **one-way, automated data flow** from physical sensors to the digital representation, characterized by:
  - **Data Transmission Protocol**: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) for lightweight, publish-subscribe messaging.
  - **Data Types**: Time-series sensor data including:
    - **Input Current (I)** as a scalar value (not explicitly quantified but implied as a floating-point representation).
    - **Torque (τ)** and **Angular Velocity (ω)** as arrays of floating-point values (e.g., 32-bit precision).
  - **Communication Frequency**: Implicitly high-frequency (time-series data implies real-time or near-real-time sampling).
  - **Event Triggers**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied to occur in response to sensor state changes or scheduled intervals.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: No explicit mention of validation/cleansing steps, but the aggregation and abstraction of raw sensor traces suggests preprocessing for noise reduction, normalization, or context enrichment.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Aggregated into a **contextual digital shadow** for specific manufacturing purposes (e.g., predictive maintenance, operational monitoring).
  The system is **passive**, meaning no real-time feedback or control commands are exchanged back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin technical connection** leverages **MQTT** for unidirectional data streaming, as explicitly stated in the document. The network topology is not detailed, but the system operates within a **cloud/edge hybrid architecture** (implied by the passive nature and potential integration with AAS), though no specific latency or bandwidth requirements are specified.

  **Key Technical Specifications**:
  - **Communication Protocol**: MQTT (for one-way data flow).
  - **Reliability/Redundancy**: Not explicitly mentioned, but the passive nature suggests minimal redundancy is required for data transmission.
  - **Security Protocols**: Not detailed; the document does not specify encryption, authentication, or access control mechanisms for the data flow.
  - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)**: Implied for structured data representation and integration, though their role in the technical connection is not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Shadow hosting and deployment** is described as an **executable system** designed to be passive, with no explicit mention of cloud, edge, or on-premise deployment environments. The document references **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** as a foundational component for the DS, suggesting a modular and potentially cloud-based deployment model.

  **Key Technical Specifications**:
  - **Hosting Environment**: Not explicitly specified (no mention of cloud/edge/on-premise), but the context implies a **scalable, real-time infrastructure** due to the streaming nature of data.
  - **Computational/Storage Requirements**: Not detailed; the document does not provide specific hardware or software constraints.
  - **Deployment Models**: Not specified; the DS is described as a standalone executable, but no mention of CI/CD pipelines, containerization (e.g., Docker/Kubernetes), or orchestration is present.
  - **Availability/Reliability**: Not detailed; the DS is passive and does not imply high-availability guarantees.
  - **Dashboard Integration**: Implied for visualization, though no specific tools (e.g., Grafana, Tableau) or deployment details are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context spans structured modeling phases aligned with **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, particularly emphasizing **multi-level abstraction** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key stages include:
  - **Development Phase**: Utilizes **SysML** and **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**, or **SysML IBDs** to define components, interfaces, and interconnections via component & connector perspectives. **Behavioral modeling** is supported through **MontiArc Automata** or **state machines** in SysML v1.7, often integrated with simulation tools such as **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages like **C++/Java**.
  - **Data Aggregation & Abstraction**: Digital Shadows (DS) serve as foundational data traces, aggregating contextual information for specific purposes, while DTs enable **active processing** (e.g., software components interpreting data) to evolve beyond passive DS roles.
  **Transition Management**: The shift from DS to DT involves integrating **active components** (e.g., software systems) to enable bidirectional data flow, governance likely follows **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SMArDT) to ensure alignment across heterogeneous stakeholders’ perspectives.
  **Lifecycle Governance**: Emphasizes **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-tool/model notation compatibility** (e.g., SysML + Simulink + C++/Java) to maintain consistency across abstraction layers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution in this context are governed by **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks, leveraging **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** and simulation tools. Key methodologies include:
  - **Engineering Processes**: Utilizes **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** to systematically model and evolve DTs across abstraction layers (e.g., SysML, AADL, MontiArc).
  - **Quality Assurance**: Implicitly relies on **MBSE validation** via ADLs (e.g., AADL’s interface validation) and simulation tools (e.g., Simulink for behavioral validation).
  - **Testing & Validation**: Combines **state machine/automaton validation** (e.g., MontiArc) with **simulation-based testing** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) to ensure functional/logical correctness.
  - **Version Control**: Not explicitly detailed, but implied through **MBSE tool integration** (e.g., collaborative modeling environments supporting traceability and revision management).
  - **Evolution Management**: DTs evolve via **active components** (e.g., software systems) enabling bidirectional data flow, transitioning from passive **Digital Shadows** (one-way data flow) to **Digital Twins** (two-way interaction).
  **Note**: No explicit version control systems or evolution-specific tools (e.g., Git for DTs) are mentioned; focus is on **MBSE methodologies** and **ADL/simulation tool chains**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The provided documents reference **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs**, which define structural and behavioral models for Digital Twin systems. **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** are mentioned in the broader context (Abstract) but are not explicitly detailed in the extracted sections. **Interoperability standards** are implied through ADLs but not formally specified. **Certification requirements** or compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are **not addressed**, and no explicit interoperability protocols (e.g., OPC UA) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates a **physical safety mechanism** for robotic operations, specifically leveraging a **safety cell** that restricts the operational range of the robotic arm (e.g., MABI Max-100) to a defined angular workspace of **[−90°, 90°]**, ensuring operator safety by preventing unintended collisions. While the provided documents do not explicitly detail cybersecurity protocols, the system’s integration with **IoT platforms** (e.g., Thingsboard) implies potential exposure to network-based threats, necessitating adherence to **MQTT protocol** (as referenced in Document 52) for secure communication between devices. The system’s compliance with **RAMI 4.0** (Document 48, 49) suggests adherence to safety-critical standards, though no explicit mention of **ISO 13849** or other cybersecurity frameworks is documented. The Digital Twin’s reliance on **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** (Document 50) for asset modeling may also imply standardized safety and security governance frameworks, though specific details on fail-safe mechanisms or threat mitigation are absent.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

It accurately focuses on friction identification and motion accuracy. However, it slightly generalizes the operational domain as 'automated assembly or manufacturing,' whereas the source specifies 'machining,' and it uses 'fault detection' instead of the specific 'friction loss detection' mentioned in the text.

Supporting quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'first axis... consisting of the motor and gearbox', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction'.

The extracted services (Friction Identification, Visualization Dashboard) are supported by the text, specifically mentioning 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' and 'visualization in a dashboard'. However, the specific target users listed ('maintenance technicians' and 'production supervisors') are not mentioned in the source documents, which only refer to 'heterogeneous stakeholders'. This constitutes an unsupported claim.

The extraction provides a comprehensive list of tools and frameworks that are well-grounded in the source, including AAS, SysML, Simulink, MQTT, and the Siemens controller. It correctly describes the digital shadow principles and data aggregation. It is marked down slightly for the unsupported claim that the SINUMERIK controller contains 'embedded real-time simulation engines' for closed-loop validation, as the text indicates simulation occurs via external MathWorks Simulink models.

Supporting quotes: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'streamed via MQTT', 'SysML', 'digital shadow... automated one-way data flow'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
